elements.sent-2411-003: We investigate the use of the Senseiver, a transformer neural network designed for sparse sensing applications, to estimate full-field surface height measurements of tsunami waves from sparse observations.
elements.sent-2411-004: The model is trained on a large ensemble of simulated data generated via a shallow water equations solver, which we show to be a faithful reproduction for the underlying dynamics by comparison to historical events.
elements.sent-2411-005: We train the model on a dataset consisting of 8 tsunami simulations whose epicenters correspond to historical USGS earthquake records, and where the model inputs are restricted to measurements obtained at actively deployed buoy locations.
elements.sent-2411-006: We test the Senseiver on a dataset consisting of 8 simulations not included in training, demonstrating its capability for extrapolation.
elements.sent-2411-007: The results show remarkable resolution of fine scale phase and amplitude features from the true field, provided that at least a few of the sensors have obtained a non-zero signal.
elements.sent-2411-008: Throughout, we discuss which forecasting techniques can be improved by this method, and suggest ways in which the flexibility of the architecture can be leveraged to incorporate arbitrary remote sensing data (eg.
elements.sent-2411-009: HF Radar and satellite measurements) as well as investigate optimal sensor placements.
elements.sent-2411-010: Accurate forecasting of tsunami waves is critical to a functioning early warning system.
elements.sent-2411-011: While physical models of tsunami waves are well-understood and solvable on a computer, the integration of such models at high resolution is computationally expensive and time consuming.
elements.sent-2411-012: The primary source of practical wave-height data is supplied by the DART (Deep-ocean Assessment and Reporting of Tsunami) Network, a series of buoys that provide wave-height measurements throughout the ocean.
elements.sent-2411-013: The challenge we address in this work is in accurately estimating how the ocean free surface evolves in space and time using only the sparse measurements obtained from the DART network.
elements.sent-2411-014: Our experiments are confined to four-hour tsunami simulations with epicenters concentrated near the coast of Japan.
elements.sent-2411-015: Within this limited regime, the number of available non-zero sensors ranges from 0 to 13.
elements.sent-2411-016: We implement and train a transformer-based neural network to reconstruct highly detailed tsunami waves from these incredibly sparse measurements.
elements.sent-2411-017: Tsunamis are a devastating natural hazard, causing more than 250,000 deaths globally between 1998 and 2017, and US$\$ 280$ billion in damages \cite{imamura2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Each year, about 60,000 people and US$\$4$billion in assets are exposed to tsunami hazard \cite{bernard2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Thus, improvements to early warning and forecast systems are highly sought and researched.
elements.sent-2411-020: The majority of existing systems employ PDE-based solvers, in which an initial ocean surface displacement is derived based on estimates of the underlying earthquake dynamics, and a shallow water type PDE model is integrated to predict the propagation of wave fronts over the ocean basin.
elements.sent-2411-021: Such approaches provide so-called `full-field' information to forecasters, including estimates of arrival time, as well as wave amplitude and shape.
elements.sent-2411-022: Various PDE-based models are currently employed both operationally and throughout the research communities, including NOAA's MOST system \cite{titov1997implementation , the GeoCLAW solver \cite{berger2011geoclaw, leveque2011tsunami , and the Gerris/Basilisk frameworks \cite{popinet2012adaptive, popinet2020vertically .}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: While such systems have produced accurate predictions for a range of historical events, integrating these models is an expensive computational task, requiring massively parallel implementations and resources due to the time-sensitive nature of the problem.
elements.sent-2411-024: The success of such systems is also tied to limitations of the associated PDE model as well as the resolution of the numerical scheme used to solve it.
elements.sent-2411-025: Due to difficulties in estimating the source and properties of seismic waves, a large class of forecasting methods seek to estimate the wave height field directly.
elements.sent-2411-026: In \cite{maeda2015 , a data assimilation method is proposed that combines bottom-pressure gauges and a 2D linear long wave equation to derive wave height and velocity estimates for forecasting.}
elements.sent-2411-027: In \cite{wang2017 , this method is made more efficient with the use of pre-computed Green's functions.}
elements.sent-2411-028: However, this method relies on a dense observation network to be effective.
elements.sent-2411-029: To address this need for dense observations, \cite{wang2019 proposed an algorithm based on the Huygens-Fresnel principal to generate virtual observation points from a sparse set of real observation points.}
elements.sent-2411-030: In \cite{fujita2024 , a sensor placement optimization procedure is combined with proper orthogonal decomposition (POD) to perform psuedo-super-resolution, yielding an artificially dense observation network.}
elements.sent-2411-031: The latter approach suffers from the reliance on a yet-to-be implemented sensor configuration, and the former suffers from overly simplistic interpolation and limitations on the density of samples.
elements.sent-2411-032: While this work is largely motivated by testing the Senseiver architecture on a variety of highly non-linear realistic phenomena, we note the above works to emphasize the importance of obtaining dense samples for existing data assimilation methods.
elements.sent-2411-033: Thus, the method presented in this work has immediate application to existing forecasting methods.
elements.sent-2411-034: We propose an attention-based machine learning approach to generate an arbitrarily dense sample of observations from sparse measurements deriving from Deep-ocean Assessment and Reporting of Tsunami Network (DART) buoys.
elements.sent-2411-035: To the best of our knowledge, the only other use of attention mechanisms in the application of sparse sensing of sea surface elevation is in \cite{archaumbalt2024 , where gap-ridden sea surface height (SSH) measurements are coupled with densely sampled sea surface temperature (SST) data to train an encoder-decoder model that generates densely sampled sea surface height reconstructions.}
elements.sent-2411-036: While this approach bears many similarities to the present work in its choice of architecture and application, the problem setup differs significantly in having sea surface elevation fields supported on lines, as well as using full resolution sea temperature fields.
elements.sent-2411-037: While the driving context of this work is in data assimilation based forecasting methods that rely on dense wave height observations, we mention a few notable uses of machine learning in tsunami forecasting.
elements.sent-2411-038: In \cite{liu2021 , machine learning techniques are used to forecast time series of tsunami wave heights at two fixed gauges in the Puget Sound.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Their forecasts are derived from wave height measurements at a hypothetical observation gauge in the Strait of Juan de Fuca.
elements.sent-2411-040: They are able to generate several hours of forecasts at the forecast gauges, given 30-60 minutes of time series data at the observation gauge.
elements.sent-2411-041: In \cite{rim2022tsunami , CNNs are trained to forecast wave heights at the same forecast gauges from Global Navigation Satellite System data.}
elements.sent-2411-042: They demonstrate 6 hours of wave height forecasts given as little as 9 minutes of GNSS data.
elements.sent-2411-043: Notably, this approach allows for the nearly instant use of input data, as there is no lag period to wait for the wave front to interact with a sensor.
elements.sent-2411-044: The use of non-pressure/wave-height measurements such as HF Radar \cite{wang2023 and distributed acoustic sensing \cite{xiao2024 is another exciting realm of forecasting.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: We develop a model based on the Senseiver architecture \cite{santos2023development , a recently developed transformer neural network designed to solve sparse sensing problems.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Rather than obtaining full-field predictions using a partial differential equation (PDE) based model, the Senseiver reconstructs such information from a set of observations distributed in space and time.
elements.sent-2411-047: To simulate the realistic application of this system to tsunami forecasting, we adopt NOAA's DART network as sensors --- a set of buoys providing high-frequency observations of ocean surface height.
elements.sent-2411-048: While the Senseiver requires only sparse inputs in order to forecast once trained, a dense set of observations are required to learn the behaviour of the system.
elements.sent-2411-049: We use a PDE-based model to generate synthetic tsunamis for given earthquake epicenters, which we sample from historical earthquake records.
elements.sent-2411-050: We assess the skill of the model in reconstructing tsunami waves for unseen events, conducting a series of experiments that assess the quality of the ocean surface height reconstructions at DART buoy locations.
elements.sent-2411-051: We also explore the effect of physics-based regularisation by adding a soft constraint during training that penalises reconstructions violating physical invariants.
elements.sent-2411-052: In the above referenced forecasting and dense sampling works, the methods are validated by comparing time series of the waveforms at fixed sensor locations.
elements.sent-2411-053: Thus, we include such time series in our assessments.
elements.sent-2411-054: We conclude by outlining future opportunities for the development of Senseiver-based forecasting systems.
elements.sent-2411-055: The tsunami forecast problem can be posed as a sparse sensing challenge: given a discrete set of observations of the ocean state $s = \{s_{1 , s_{2 , \dots, s_{N}\}$ at locations $\mathbf{x ^{s = \{\mathbf{x ^{s _{1 , \mathbf{x ^{s _{2 , \dots, \mathbf{x ^{s _{N}\}$ and time $t$, reconstruct a set of state outputs $\hat{s = \{\hat{s _{1 , \hat{s _{2 , \dots, \hat{s _{M}\}$ at a set of query points $\mathbf{x ^{q = \{\mathbf{x ^{q _{1 , \mathbf{x ^{q _{2 , \dots, \mathbf{x ^{q _{M}\}$ and query times $t^{q \geq t$ such that $M \gg N$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: In this work, the input state $s(\mathbf{x ^{s , t)$ is taken to be the ocean surface height measured at DART buoy locations, and the query state $\hat{s (\mathbf{x ^{q , t^{q )$ is the ocean surface height throughout the remainder of the global ocean domain and at future times $t^{q}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Noting that DART buoys number only a few dozen, and that even relatively coarse coverage of the global ocean requires at least several hundred thousand discrete points, the reconstruction problem is seen to be extremely sparse.
elements.sent-2411-058: Recently, the Senseiver \cite{santos2023development , an attention-based transformer neural network, has been developed to solve such problems --- employing a multi-level encoder-decoder architecture to reconstruct dense output from sparse observations.}
elements.sent-2411-059: In brief, the model operates as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-060: A positional encoder $P_{E}$ is used to map the location of observations to a spatial encoding vector $\mathbf{a ^{s}$, with $s \in \mathbb{R ^{N}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: An attention-based encoder $E$ is used to map the encoded observation/location pairs $(s_{i , \mathbf{a ^{s _{i )$ to a latent matrix $\mathbf{Z}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: The positional encoder $P_{E}$ is used to map query locations to a spatial encoding vector $\mathbf{a ^{q}$, with $q \in \mathbb{R ^{M , M \gg N$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: An attention-based decoder $D$ is used to reconstruct outputs at the encoded query locations $\mathbf{a ^{q}$.}
elements.sent-2411-064: The positional encoder $P_{E}$ implements a trigonometric encoding of the spatial coordinates, and the encoder and decoder operators $E$, $D$ contain trainable layers in the form of multi-layer perceptrons within their attention blocks.
elements.sent-2411-065: The reader is referred to \cite{santos2023development for in-depth discussions of the Senseiver architecture, summarised here as follows \begin{equation}a^{s} = P_{E}(x^{s})\,, [2ex] Z = E(s, a^{s})\,, [2ex] a^{q} = P_{E}(x^{q})\,, [2ex] \hat{s}(x^{q}, t^{q}) = D(Z, a^{q})\,.\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-066: While the Senseiver is capable of reconstructing dense output from sparse observations, it must first learn the behaviour of the system through a training procedure in which the weights of the model are optimised by minimising an error measure, here taken to be the mean-squared error \begin{equation}\label{eqn:training_loss} L = \sum \Big( s(x^{q}, t^{q}) - \hat{s}(x^{q}, t^{q})\Big)^2\,, [2ex] where\quad \hat{s}(x^{q}, t^{q}) = D\left( E\left(s(x^{q},t^{q}), P_{E}(x^{s})\right), P_E(x^{q}) \right)\,.\end{equation}.

elements.section-2411-005: Summary of Model Training and Experiments
elements.sent-2411-097: We trained a total of five models, each composed of eight epicenters.
elements.sent-2411-098: For the mesh used in the SWE solver, each frame is output as an unstructured list of 163,842 tuples, consisting of a longitude/latitude coordinate and a wave height.
elements.sent-2411-099: We subsample each frame by a factor of 2, yielding frames consisting of 81,921 pixels.
elements.sent-2411-100: For each training epicenter, the simulation is run for 144 time steps, with a time step of 50s, effectively giving 2 hour simulations.
elements.sent-2411-101: For all models we used a 95/5 test/train split.
elements.sent-2411-102: The epicenter coordinates used in training were randomly selected from a list of seismic events restricted to the coast of Japan.
elements.sent-2411-103: The models are distinguished by two main attributes: (1) time regime and (2) physical regularization.
elements.sent-2411-104: Since we consider the time regimes of 0-2 hours and 2-4 hours, and both regularized and unregularized training procedures, four models are distinguished.
elements.sent-2411-105: The fifth model concerns the 0-2 hour time regime, and is distinguished by the addition of three artificially placed sensors.
elements.sent-2411-106: This model serves as the basis of a proof-of-concept for future Senseiver experiments to explore optimal sensor placement to improve reconstructions with particularly limited sensor information.
elements.sent-2411-107: The weights of the unregularized model are optimized to minimize the mean square error over a batch of data, with the squared error given by \begin{equation}\label{eqn:loss function} \mathcal{L} = \left( s(x_q,t_q) - D\left(E\left(P(x_s,t_s),s(x_q,t_q), PE(x_q)\right) \right)\right)^2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-108: The batch is defined by hyper parameters determining number of frames and number of pixels per frame.
elements.sent-2411-109: The algorithm to minimize the mean squared error is Adam, with learning rate $1 \times 10^{-4}$.
elements.sent-2411-110: Beyond the baseline validation on the training data, we conducted two additional experiments to test the Senseiver reconstructions.
elements.sent-2411-111: We perturbed the eight training epicenters to probe the extrapolatory capability of the model.
elements.sent-2411-112: We emphasize that none of the frames of these perturbed epicenter simulations are included in the training set.
elements.sent-2411-113: We refer to this data as the unseen epicenters.
elements.sent-2411-114: These epicenters are depicted visually in relation to the training epicenters in Figure~\ref{fig:train_unseeen_epis .}
elements.sent-2411-115: The distances of the unseen epicenters from their closest training epicenter ranges from 2.78 miles to 119.97 miles.
elements.sent-2411-116: To assess the physical consistency of the reconstructions, we train models with a regularization penalty based on the SWE continuity equation (8) and compare the results to the unregularized models.
elements.sent-2411-117: The error metric we use to evaluate our models is scaled by the absolute max wave height for each frame, and is computed only over pixels achieving a value of at least $1\times 10^{-4}$ in the true field.
elements.sent-2411-118: This pixel restriction mitigates the issue of artificially low error due to most of the wave height field over the ocean being identically 0.
elements.sent-2411-119: For each frame we compute the absolute difference between the true and reconstructed wave height fields, and divide by the maximum absolute true wave height for that frame.
elements.sent-2411-121: In the discussion section, we refer to \cite{marcato2023reconstruction , where a training procedure is implemented that allows the Senseiver to deduce optimal sensor placements.}
elements.sent-2411-122: To enhance this discussion, we include the results of an experiment where the training data is augmented with artificial sensors.

elements.sent-2411-222: We investigate the potential of an attention-based neural network architecture known as the Senseiver to perform sparse sensing tasks in the context of tsunami forecasting.
elements.sent-2411-223: In particular, we focus on the Tsunami Data Assimilation Method, where forecasts are derived from tsunameter networks.
elements.sent-2411-224: We used our model to generate high-resolution tsunami waves from incredibly sparse observations, whose epicenters are not included in the training set.
elements.sent-2411-225: We also show significantly improved accuracy in the generation of dense observation networks by comparison to the Linear Interpolation with Huygens-Fresnel Principle.
elements.sent-2411-226: While machine learning methods have been used to provide accurate forecasts of tsunami waveforms at fixed observation points, full-field reconstruction of tsunami waves from sparse observations have yet to be demonstrated by machine learning methods.
elements.sent-2411-227: This task is relevant to the tsunami data assimilation method, where in-ocean tsunameter observations are combined with numerical methods to forecast tsunamis.
elements.sent-2411-228: In this work, we use an attention-based neural network architecture known as the Senseiver to generate remarkable tsunami wave reconstructions given extremely sparse tsunameter measurements corresponding to real sensor locations.
elements.sent-2411-229: To demonstrate the utility of our method for forecasters using the tsunami data assimilation method, we conduct experiments where the Senseiver is used to generate virtual waveforms in a sparse observation network, and compare the results to an established interpolation method used for the same task.
elements.sent-2411-230: Tsunami early warning systems are important tools for minimizing the human and economic costs of tsunamis.
elements.sent-2411-231: The majority of existing systems employ partial differential equation (PDE) based solvers, in which an initial ocean surface displacement is derived based on estimates of the underlying earthquake dynamics, and a shallow water equation (SWE) type PDE model is integrated to predict the propagation of wave fronts over the ocean basin.
elements.sent-2411-232: Various PDE-based models are currently employed both operationally and throughout the research communities, including NOAA's MOST system \cite{titov1997implementation , the GeoCLAW solver \cite{berger2011geoclaw,leveque2011tsunami , and the Gerris/Basilisk frameworks \cite{popinet2012adaptive,popinet2020vertically .}}}
elements.sent-2411-233: These methods suffer from uncertainties in the estimation of seismic parameters, as well as steep computational costs in integrating the PDEs.
elements.sent-2411-234: The tsunami data assimilation method (TDAM) \cite{maeda2015 was introduced to mitigate these issues.}
elements.sent-2411-235: In TDAM, tsunameter observations are combined with 2d Linear Long Wave equations (LLW) to forecast arrival times and wave amplitudes at selected observation gauges, bypasing the needs for source inversion and expensive integration of PDEs.
elements.sent-2411-236: Although TDAM is effective in regions of the ocean with dense observation networks, its accuracy is limited in sparse observation networks.
elements.sent-2411-237: In such regions, there is a need for sparse sensing capabilities to make TDAM a practical tool for forecasters.
elements.sent-2411-238: Although machine learning (ML) methods have been proposed to provide tsunami waveform forecasts at fixed observation gauges \cite{liu2021 ,\cite{rim2022tsunami , to the best of our knowledge no ML-based solutions have been proposed for sparse sensing problems in the context of tsunami waves.}}
elements.sent-2411-239: In this paper, we explore the use of the Senseiver, an attention-based neural network designed for sparse sensing tasks, to reconstruct high resolution tsunami waves from sparse tsunameter obsevations.
elements.sent-2411-240: We also test the use of the Senseiver in generating dense observation networks and compare its performance against the Linear Interpolation with Huygens-Fresnel Principle (LIHFP), proposed in \cite{wang2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-241: Our experiments are set up to mimic a forecasting workflow where a pretrained model is used to reconstruct full-field tsunami waves from measurements of the Deep-ocean Assessment and Reporting of Tsunami Network (DART) buoys.
elements.sent-2411-242: Our model is tested on measurements whose tsunami epicenters differ from those used in the training set, showcasing the Senseiver's capability to extrapolate to initial conditions not seen in training.
elements.sent-2411-243: In addition to evaluating the $L^{1}$ error and physical consistency of high-resolution reconstructions, we demonstrate an improvement in the generation of virtual waveforms at artificial observation points compared to the LIHFP method.
elements.sent-2411-244: In particular, the virtual waveforms recovered by our method exhibit approximately a two-fold decrease in average wave height, arrival time, and maximum amplitude errors.
elements.sent-2411-245: In the conclusion, we recommend future experiments to probe the utility of the Senseiver in related forecasting problems, such as direct forecasting of high-resolution tsunami waves from tsunameters, early warning systems from GNSS data, and optimal sensor placements.

elements.sent-2411-259: The Senseiver \cite{santos2023development , an attention-based neural network, has been developed to solve such problems --- employing a multi-level encoder-decoder architecture to reconstruct dense output from sparse observations.}
elements.sent-2411-260: The core concept of the Senseiver is to combine the attention operation \cite{Vaswani2017AttentionIA with data compression.}
elements.sent-2411-261: Some key attributes of the attention operation that make it powerful for sparse sensing tasks are: (1) it treats positional information as a feature, (2) it is grid agnostic, making it amenable to data on arbitrary mesh structures, and (3) it can make long-range spatial associations immediately (unlike CNNs where spatially distinct elements are not combined until very deep in the network).
elements.sent-2411-262: The cost of these capabilities is that the attention operation scales quadratically with the size of its input.
elements.sent-2411-263: The Senseiver mitigates this computational bottleneck by encoding the input into latent arrays of reduced size, wherein the bulk of attention operations occur.

elements.sent-2411-329: We investigate the potential of an attention-based neural network architecture, the Senseiver, for sparse sensing in tsunami forecasting.
elements.sent-2411-330: Specifically, we focus on the Tsunami Data Assimilation Method, which generates forecasts from tsunameter networks.
elements.sent-2411-331: Our model is used to reconstruct high-resolution tsunami wavefields from extremely sparse observations, including cases where the tsunami epicenters are not represented in the training set.
elements.sent-2411-332: Furthermore, we demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms the Linear Interpolation with Huygens-Fresnel Principle in generating dense observation networks, achieving markedly improved accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-333: While machine learning methods have achieved accurate forecasts of tsunami waveforms at fixed observation points, the full-field reconstruction of tsunami waves from sparse observations has not yet been demonstrated using machine learning techniques.
elements.sent-2411-334: This challenge is highly relevant to tsunami data assimilation, where in-ocean tsunameter observations are integrated with numerical models to improve tsunami forecasts.
elements.sent-2411-335: In this study, we employ an attention-based neural network architecture, the Senseiver, to produce high-fidelity reconstructions of tsunami wavefields from extremely sparse tsunameter measurements at realistic sensor locations.
elements.sent-2411-336: To illustrate the practical value of our approach for tsunami forecasters, we perform experiments in which the Senseiver generates virtual waveforms within a sparse observation network, and we compare its performance to a widely used interpolation method for the same task.
elements.sent-2411-337: Tsunami early warning systems are essential for reducing the human and economic impacts of these devastating natural events.
elements.sent-2411-338: Most operational systems rely on partial differential equation (PDE)-based solvers, where the initial ocean surface displacement is estimated from earthquake dynamics and a shallow water equation (SWE) model is integrated to predict tsunami propagation.
elements.sent-2411-339: Widely used frameworks include NOAA’s MOST system \cite{Titov2016,titov1997implementation , GeoCLAW \cite{berger2011geoclaw,leveque2011tsunami , and Gerris/Basilisk \cite{popinet2012adaptive,popinet2020vertically .}}}
elements.sent-2411-340: Despite their broad adoption, these approaches face challenges: uncertainties in seismic parameter estimation, high computational cost, and limited real-time adaptability.
elements.sent-2411-341: To address these challenges, operational forecasting increasingly leverages sparse offshore and onshore observations for rapid source characterization and wave prediction.
elements.sent-2411-342: Methods such as DART buoy data inversion \cite{percival2011 , direct energy estimation from deep-ocean pressure measurements \cite{bernard2013 , and real-time data assimilation using GNSS and other sensors \cite{Hiroaki2014 have proven effective for improving forecast speed and accuracy.}}}
elements.sent-2411-343: However, these approaches typically rely on statistical or physics-based methods and do not employ machine learning (ML) architectures.
elements.sent-2411-344: Recent ML approaches have shown promise for forecasting tsunami waveforms at fixed points \cite{liu2021, rim2022tsunami , but, to our knowledge, no ML-based solutions have addressed the sparse sensing problem for reconstructing full tsunami wavefields.}
elements.sent-2411-345: Here, we introduce Senseiver, an attention-based neural network tailored for sparse sensing, capable of reconstructing high-resolution tsunami wavefields from limited tsunameter observations.
elements.sent-2411-346: We benchmark Senseiver against the Linear Interpolation with Huygens-Fresnel Principle (LIHFP) method \cite{wang2019 , evaluating both reconstruction accuracy and the generation of virtual waveforms at unobserved locations.}
elements.sent-2411-347: Our experiments emulate an operational workflow, reconstructing full-field tsunami waves from DART buoy measurements, and assess generalization to unseen epicenters.
elements.sent-2411-348: Results show that Senseiver achieves approximately a two-fold reduction in average errors for wave height, arrival time, and maximum amplitude compared to LIHFP, highlighting the potential of attention-based ML for advancing tsunami early warning capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-350: This idealized, synthetic setup was chosen to simplify both model training and subsequent analysis.
elements.sent-2411-351: The 5-meter initial wave height is intended to represent significant, though not extreme, tsunami-generating events.
elements.sent-2411-352: Incorporating simulations with varying amplitudes and wavelengths is left for future work.
elements.sent-2411-353: For details on the numerical methods, including calibration and validation of the PDE model using historical DART data, see Texts S1–S2 and Figures S1–S2 in the Supporting Information.
elements.sent-2411-354: To construct the training dataset, we selected 11 epicenters from USGS earthquake records \cite{usgs_earthquake_map , filtering for events with location descriptions containing "Japan" and magnitudes of at least 7.5.}
elements.sent-2411-355: For the test set, we identified an additional 11 epicenters from the USGS records, ensuring each test epicenter lies within 20 to 100 miles of at least one training epicenter.
elements.sent-2411-356: This selection strategy emulates a realistic scenario: the model is trained on a diverse set of historical events from a specific region, while inference is performed on new events whose initial conditions are unknown but geographically and seismologically similar to those in training.
elements.sent-2411-357: Figure~\ref{fig:train_unseeen_epis provides a visual overview of the training and test epicenters, all located between 136 and 145 degrees longitude and 33 and 43 degrees latitude.}
elements.sent-2411-358: This region is sufficiently broad to capture a wide range of tsunami dynamics, while ensuring that test cases remain reasonably close to the training set.
elements.sent-2411-359: The minimum and maximum distances between each test epicenter and its nearest training epicenter range from 22.65 to 90.55 miles; detailed values are provided in Table~\ref{table:table , and a full matrix of all test–train pairwise distances is included in Table S1 of the Supporting Information.}
elements.sent-2411-360: For the mesh used in our SWE solver, each simulation frame is represented as an unstructured list of 163,842 tuples, each consisting of a longitude/latitude coordinate and a corresponding wave height.
elements.sent-2411-361: To reduce data size, we subsample each frame by a factor of two, resulting in frames with 81,921 pixels.
elements.sent-2411-362: For each epicenter, simulations are run for 289 time steps at 50-second intervals, yielding a total simulated duration of four hours.
elements.sent-2411-363: The 50-second interval was chosen not only to capture tsunami dynamics at high temporal resolution, but also to enable accurate second-order finite difference diagnostics for physical consistency checks (see Supporting Information, Text S3).

elements.sent-2411-365: The sparse sensing problem for tsunami waves can be formulated as follows: Given a discrete set of ocean state observations $s = \{s_{1 , s_{2 , \dots, s_{N}\}$ collected at locations $\mathbf{x ^{s = \{\mathbf{x ^{s _{1 , \mathbf{x ^{s _{2 , \dots, \mathbf{x ^{s _{N}\}$, the objective is to reconstruct the state $\hat{s = \{\hat{s _{1 , \hat{s _{2 , \dots, \hat{s _{M}\}$ at a set of query points $\mathbf{x ^{q = \{\mathbf{x ^{q _{1 , \mathbf{x ^{q _{2 , \dots, \mathbf{x ^{q _{M}\}$ where typically $M \gg N$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-366: In our work, the input state $s(\mathbf{x ^{s )$ corresponds to ocean surface heights measured at DART buoy locations, while the query state $\hat{s (\mathbf{x ^{q )$ represents the ocean surface height throughout the global ocean domain.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-367: Since the DART network consists of only a few dozen buoys, yet even coarse global coverage requires hundreds of thousands of points, this reconstruction task is extremely sparse.
elements.sent-2411-368: To address this challenge, we employ the Senseiver \cite{santos2023development , an attention-based neural network specifically designed for sparse-to-dense reconstruction tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-369: The Senseiver utilizes a multi-level encoder-decoder architecture that leverages the strengths of attention mechanisms and data compression.
elements.sent-2411-370: Key advantages of the attention operation for sparse sensing include: (1) treating positional information as a feature, (2) being agnostic to grid structure, thus accommodating arbitrary meshes, and (3) enabling immediate long-range spatial associations-unlike CNNs, which require deep layers to integrate distant features.
elements.sent-2411-371: However, attention operations scale quadratically with input size.
elements.sent-2411-372: Senseiver overcomes this bottleneck by encoding the input into compact latent arrays, where most attention computations are performed.
elements.sent-2411-373: The Senseiver workflow is as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-374: A positional encoder $P_E$ maps observation locations to spatial encoding vectors $\mathbf{a ^s$.}
elements.sent-2411-375: An attention-based encoder $E$ transforms the observation-value/location pairs $(s_i, \mathbf{a ^s_i)$ into a latent matrix $\mathbf{Z}$.}
elements.sent-2411-376: The positional encoder $P_E$ also maps query locations to spatial encodings $\mathbf{a ^q$.}
elements.sent-2411-377: An attention-based decoder $D$ reconstructs outputs at the encoded query locations $\mathbf{a ^q$.}
elements.sent-2411-378: The positional encoder $P_E$ implements a trigonometric encoding of spatial coordinates, while the encoder and decoder ($E$, $D$) incorporate trainable multi-layer perceptrons within their attention blocks.
elements.sent-2411-379: For a comprehensive description of the Senseiver architecture, see \cite{santos2023development .}
elements.sent-2411-380: The process can be summarized as: \begin{equation}a^{s} = P_{E}(x^{s})\,, [2ex] Z = E(s, a^{s})\,, [2ex] a^{q} = P_{E}(x^{q})\,, [2ex] \hat{s}(x^{q}) = D(Z, a^{q})\,.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}a^{s} = P_{E}(x^{s})\,, [2ex] Z = E(s, a^{s})\,, [2ex] a^{q} = P_{E}(x^{q})\,, [2ex] \hat{s}(x^{q}) = D(Z, a^{q})\,.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-381: This flexible architecture enables modeling on unstructured data and allows for custom coordinate choices in the positional encoder.
elements.sent-2411-382: In our application, we augment latitude and longitude with ocean bathymetry encodings.
elements.sent-2411-383: Land pixels are masked out, ensuring training occurs only on ocean data.
elements.sent-2411-384: A schematic of the Senseiver workflow is presented in Figure~\ref{fig:sens_workflow .}
elements.sent-2411-385: Model weights are optimized by minimizing the mean squared error: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:training_loss} L = \sum \Big( s(x^{q}) - \hat{s}(x^{q})\Big)^2\,, [2ex] where\quad \hat{s}(x^{q}) = D\left( E\left(s(x^{q}), P_{E}(x^{s})\right), P_E(x^{q}) \right),\end{equation} using the Adam optimizer \cite{kingma2014adam .}
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:training_loss} L = \sum \Big( s(x^{q}) - \hat{s}(x^{q})\Big)^2\,, [2ex] where\quad \hat{s}(x^{q}) = D\left( E\left(s(x^{q}), P_{E}(x^{s})\right), P_E(x^{q}) \right),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-386: To prevent overfitting, we train on only $80\%$ of the available training frames.
elements.sent-2411-387: The number and ordering of query points, as well as the data frames used in training, are treated as hyperparameters, with further details provided in Text S4 of the Supporting Information.
elements.figure-2411-015: \includegraphics[width=1.05\textwidth]{fig2.png} \caption{Overview of the Senseiver architecture for tsunami reconstruction. The sensor values along with their encoded positions are processed by the encoder into a latent array $Z$ of fixed size. The encoded query locations are processed by the attention-based decoder, which outputs an estimate of wave height at the query location(s).} \label{fig:sens_workflow}
elements.sent-2411-388: The primary error metric used to evaluate reconstruction quality is the mean absolute error (MAE), normalized by the absolute maximum wave height in each frame.
elements.sent-2411-389: To ensure this metric captures meaningful differences, we compute the MAE only over pixels where the true wave height exceeds $1 \times 10^{-4}$.
elements.sent-2411-390: This threshold is necessary because most of the ocean domain has zero wave height; including these pixels would artificially deflate the error.
elements.sent-2411-391: For each reconstruction, we calculate the absolute difference between the true and reconstructed wave height fields, divided by the maximum absolute true wave height for that frame: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:recon_loss} \text{Error}(x,y,t) = \frac{|h(x,y,t)-\hat{h}(x,y,t)|}{\text{max}(|h(x,y,t)|)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-392: While this metric provides a standard quantitative assessment typical in machine learning, we also sought to evaluate our approach in a more operationally relevant context.
elements.sent-2411-393: In practice, tsunami forecasting systems such as the tsunami data assimilation method (TDAM) \cite{maeda2015 focus on predicting key features of tsunami waveforms—specifically, arrival times and maximum amplitudes—at fixed observation points.}
elements.sent-2411-394: TDAM performs well with dense observation networks, but its accuracy diminishes with sparse sensor coverage.
elements.sent-2411-395: To address this, the Linear Interpolation with Huygens-Fresnel Principle (LIHFP) \cite{wang2019 was developed to augment sparse networks, estimating virtual waveforms at unsensed locations by interpolating between real sensor data while accounting for arrival times, distances, and bathymetry.}
elements.sent-2411-396: To bridge the gap between standard ML evaluation and practical tsunami forecasting, we compare our model’s reconstructions not only in terms of full-field MAE, but also by generating six virtual waveforms at strategically chosen locations and benchmarking them against those produced by the LIHFP method.
elements.sent-2411-397: This dual evaluation strategy allows us to assess both the general reconstruction capability of our model and its effectiveness in a real-world forecasting workflow.
elements.sent-2411-398: Averaging the reconstruction loss (Eq.~\ref{eqn:recon_loss ) over all 289 frames from each of the eleven test epicenters yields a global experiment error of $5.00 \times 10^{-2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-399: Notably, much of this error arises from the early time frames, when sensors have yet to detect significant tsunami signals.
elements.sent-2411-400: This limitation is inherent to any assimilation method that relies solely on tsunameter data.
elements.sent-2411-401: On average, the reconstruction error remains below $1 \times 10^{-1}$ after 32.9 minutes, with a median threshold time of 38.3 minutes.
elements.sent-2411-402: To assess the physical consistency of our model’s reconstructions, we evaluated the continuity equation residual (Eq.~\ref{eqn:continuity ), yielding a global average error of $4.10 \times 10^{-2}$ across all times and epicenters.}

elements.sent-2411-414: To ensure consistency with the experimental setup of \cite{wang2019 and enable direct comparison, we focused our evaluation on virtual waveform reconstruction at midpoints between selected DART buoys.}
elements.sent-2411-415: Although our experiments center on these midpoints, the methodology is readily extensible to arbitrary virtual sensor locations.
elements.sent-2411-416: In fact, analyzing Senseiver performance as the virtual observation point moves farther from the training data may provide valuable insight into the method’s effective range—potentially revealing where LIHFP waveforms surpass Senseiver reconstructions in accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-417: For this study, we identified six midpoints among DART buoys that registered significant waveforms in our simulations and compared the Senseiver’s reconstructions at these locations to those produced by LIHFP.
elements.sent-2411-418: Across the majority of test epicenters, times, and virtual observation sites, the Senseiver outperformed LIHFP, achieving a mean absolute error (MAE) in wave height of $7.02 \times 10^{-2}$ meters, compared to $18.2 \times 10^{-2}$ meters for LIHFP.
elements.sent-2411-419: The Senseiver also demonstrated substantial improvements in estimating both arrival times and maximum amplitudes at nearly all virtual observation points.
elements.sent-2411-420: Specifically, the mean absolute error for arrival time, averaged across all eleven simulations, was $8.12$ minutes for the Senseiver and $16.68$ minutes for LIHFP.

elements.sent-2411-431: We conclude this section by noting the lack of temporal smoothness observed in the waveforms generated by the Senseiver.
elements.sent-2411-432: To mitigate this issue, we applied median filtering—a non-linear technique that replaces each point in a signal with the median value within a local window, effectively reducing noise and outliers while preserving key features—in our virtual waveform experiments.
elements.sent-2411-433: This post-processing step substantially improved arrival time accuracy: for arrival times defined by a 3 cm threshold, repeated median filtering reduced the raw Senseiver error from $9.05$ minutes to $5.35$ minutes.

elements.sent-2411-438: We have presented Senseiver, an attention-based neural network capable of reconstructing high-resolution tsunami wavefields from sparse observation networks.
elements.sent-2411-439: Our results demonstrate substantial improvements over the LIHFP baseline, particularly in generating accurate virtual waveforms at unobserved locations.
elements.sent-2411-440: To our knowledge, this is the first ML-based solution to address the sparse sensing problem for tsunami forecasting at high spatial resolution.
elements.sent-2411-441: This work serves as a proof of concept, with several important limitations.
elements.sent-2411-442: Our experiments are restricted to a limited oceanic region and idealized initial conditions.
elements.sent-2411-443: Future research should extend these methods to broader domains, diversify training data, and investigate how regional scale interacts with model complexity.
elements.sent-2411-444: Incorporating additional features and regularization strategies may further improve the temporal smoothness of Senseiver-generated waveforms.
elements.sent-2411-445: A key challenge remains early-stage reconstruction, before tsunameter data are available.
elements.sent-2411-446: Future work could explore integrating alternative data sources such as GNSS \cite{rim2022tsunami , HF radar \cite{wang2023 , or distributed acoustic sensing \cite{xiao2024 to provide earlier or more robust wavefield estimates.}}}
elements.sent-2411-447: Additionally, adapting Senseiver to optimize sensor placement during training \cite{marcato2023reconstruction could inform the design of observation networks, particularly in resource-limited regions.}
elements.sent-2411-448: By bridging advances in machine learning with operational tsunami sensing, Senseiver offers a promising step toward more accurate and timely tsunami early warning systems.
elements.sent-2411-449: The tsunami simulation data used in this study are available from Zenodo \url{https://zenodo.org/records/15478821 \cite{McDugald2025TsunamiSims .}}
elements.sent-2411-450: The Senseiver codebase is available from GitHub \url{https://github.com/OrchardLANL/Senseiver \cite{Santos2023Senseiver , with tsunami-specific code provided in the ``tsunami'' directory.}}
elements.sent-2411-451: Formal citations for both the dataset and software are included in the reference list.
elements.sent-2411-452: Andrew Arnold (University of Arizona PhD Student in Applied Mathematics) assisted in analyzing the virtual waveform experiments.. \FloatBarrier.
elements.sent-2411-453: Tsunami early warning systems are critical for mitigating the devastating human and economic impacts of these natural hazards.
elements.sent-2411-454: Most operational systems rely on partial differential equation (PDE)-based solvers, where the initial ocean surface displacement is inferred from earthquake source parameters and then propagated using shallow water equation (SWE) models.
elements.sent-2411-455: Widely adopted frameworks include NOAA’s MOST system \cite{Titov2016,titov1997implementation , GeoClaw \cite{berger2011geoclaw,leveque2011tsunami , and Gerris/Basilisk \cite{popinet2012adaptive,popinet2020vertically .}}}
elements.sent-2411-456: Despite broad use, these approaches face significant challenges, including uncertainty in seismic parameter estimation, high computational demand, and limited real-time adaptability.
elements.sent-2411-457: To overcome these limitations and enable rapid response, tsunami forecasting increasingly leverages sparse offshore and onshore observations for source characterization and direct wave prediction.
elements.sent-2411-458: Methods such as DART buoy data inversion \cite{percival2011 , direct energy estimation from deep-ocean pressure measurements \cite{bernard2013 , and real-time data assimilation using GNSS and other sensors \cite{Hiroaki2014 have improved speed and accuracy, yet perform best when observation networks are sufficiently dense and well-placed.}}}
elements.sent-2411-459: This has motivated the development of sparse sensing strategies to enhance observational coverage through techniques such as the generation of virtual waveform observations and optimization of sensor placement.
elements.sent-2411-460: For example, Wang et al.'s \cite{wang2019 Huygens-Fresnel interpolation synthesizes virtual tsunami waveforms from sparse tsunameter observations, effectively constructing a denser network that can then be used as input to data assimilation schemes, such as optimal interpolation \cite{maeda2015 .}}
elements.sent-2411-461: Concurrently, sensor placement optimization has become an active research area, with Fujita et al. \cite{fujita2024 combining sensor placement and proper orthogonal decomposition for pseudo-super-resolution, and Wang et al. \cite{wang2020 applying empirical orthogonal function (EOF) analysis to optimally deploy gauges around Crete Island for maximal early warning capability.}}
elements.sent-2411-462: Notably, these methods are rooted primarily in statistical or physics-based frameworks and have rarely incorporated machine learning (ML) architectures for wavefield reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-463: Beyond forecasting arrival times and amplitudes at fixed locations, there is increasing recognition that capturing the spatial dynamics of tsunami waves over wide areas is essential.
elements.sent-2411-464: This not only improves hazard understanding but also supports emergency response, a need highlighted in post-disaster damage assessments using remote sensing and image analysis techniques \cite{koshimura2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-465: Recent ML approaches show promise in forecasting tsunami waveforms at fixed points \cite{liu2021, rim2022tsunami , but ML solutions for reconstructing full tsunami wavefields from sparse data remain underdeveloped.}
elements.sent-2411-466: A related advancement by Archambault et al. \cite{archaumbalt2024 used an attention-based encoder-decoder model to reconstruct sea surface height fields from incomplete satellite altimetry data, leveraging auxiliary sea surface temperature observations and a supervised-to-unsupervised fine-tuning strategy.}
elements.sent-2411-467: While their architecture is similar to ours, their problem focuses on spatial interpolation of gridded satellite data, rather than sparse, point-based tsunami sensor networks.
elements.sent-2411-468: Here, we adapt Senseiver \cite{Santos2023Senseiver , an attention-based neural network tailored for sparse sensing, capable of reconstructing high-resolution tsunami wavefields from limited tsunameter observations.}
elements.sent-2411-469: To focus on the fundamental capabilities of this architecture, we adopt an idealized simulation environment that uses parameterized source models rather than fault-derived displacements.
elements.sent-2411-470: We benchmark Senseiver against the Linear Interpolation with Huygens-Fresnel Principle (LIHFP) method \cite{wang2019 , evaluating accuracy and virtual waveform generation at unobserved locations.}
elements.sent-2411-471: Our experiments emulate an operational-like workflow using realistic DART buoy configurations and test generalization to previously unseen epicenters.
elements.sent-2411-472: In addition to generating remarkable full-field reconstructions, results show that Senseiver reduces average errors in wave height, arrival time, and maximum amplitude by approximately a factor of two compared to LIHFP---demonstrating the promise of attention-based ML tools for advancing sensor-informed tsunami forecasting and hazard assessment.
elements.sent-2411-474: This approach avoids the complexity of explicitly modeling earthquake fault slip---a technically demanding problem and an active area of research~\cite{nhess2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-475: While more detailed source models are possible (e.g., elastic dislocation or finite fault modeling), adopting a parameterization based solely on epicenter location enables simplicity of experiment design and subsequent analysis.
elements.sent-2411-476: The 5-meter displacement represents a significant yet plausible event, consistent with historically observed tsunami amplitudes.
elements.sent-2411-477: Importantly, this work is structured as a proof of concept for the Senseiver sparse sensing framework.
elements.sent-2411-478: Our primary goal is to evaluate whether Senseiver can reconstruct high-resolution tsunami wavefields from sparse observations, using the deep-ocean DART system as a realistic baseline.
elements.sent-2411-479: DART buoys were selected specifically because they span broad offshore regions, enabling us to assess Senseiver’s capacity to infer wavefields over large spatial domains—potentially enhancing direct forecasting and improving data assimilation schemes through virtual waveform augmentation, as demonstrated in~\cite{wang2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-480: To construct our training and test datasets, we queried the USGS earthquake catalog~\cite{usgs_earthquake_map , filtering for events that (a) had magnitudes $\geq$ 7.5, (b) listed ``Japan'' in the location descriptor, and (c) occurred in water depths greater than 1000 meters.}
elements.sent-2411-481: The depth constraint was adopted to avoid simulation artifacts from initial conditions placed too close to the coast, which can introduce artifacts on coarser meshes.
elements.sent-2411-482: This also ensures the events used are in operationally relevant offshore regions where DART buoys can realistically contribute data.
elements.sent-2411-483: From an initial pool of 92 qualifying events meeting our criteria, we randomly selected 11 epicenters for training.
elements.sent-2411-484: Test epicenters were drawn from the same pool, ensuring each lies within 20 to 100 miles of a training source, to emulate scenarios where events are seismologically related but not spatially redundant.
elements.sent-2411-485: This strategy achieves a balance between data diversity and realistic generalization: the model is exposed during training to a broad distribution of real-world offshore earthquakes, while inference is performed on novel but physically consistent examples.
elements.sent-2411-486: All selected epicenters (see Figure~\ref{fig:train_unseeen_epis ) are located between $136^\circ$–$145^\circ$ longitude and $33^\circ$–$43^\circ$ latitude.}
elements.sent-2411-487: The test events lie 20–100 miles from the nearest training epicenter, emulating unseen but seismologically relevant cases.
elements.sent-2411-488: A full pairwise distance matrix is provided in Table~S1.
elements.sent-2411-489: We acknowledge that for typical operational tsunami forecasting near Japan—especially for events generated close to the coast—DART buoy data alone may not be sufficient due to short arrival times.
elements.sent-2411-490: In practice, such forecasts often rely on coastal instrumentation and denser observation networks.
elements.sent-2411-491: Nevertheless, our focus is not on coastal arrival prediction in isolation, but on evaluating the viability of Senseiver in general settings where sparse observations are the primary inputs.
elements.sent-2411-492: We believe Senseiver’s demonstrated success across a realistic DART configuration strongly suggests it would also perform well in other settings, including denser local arrays and finer model meshes.
elements.sent-2411-493: Each simulation frame consists of 163,842 unstructured spatial points (longitude, latitude, wave height).
elements.sent-2411-494: To reduce computational load, we subsample frames by a factor of two, resulting in $81,921$ pixels per time step.
elements.sent-2411-495: Simulations span 4 hours, computed at 50-second intervals (289 time steps), yielding a dataset of size $(3179, 81921)$ for each of the training and test sets.
elements.sent-2411-496: Temporal resolution is chosen to balance signal fidelity with computational cost and supports second-order finite difference diagnostics in model validation (see Supporting Information, Text S3).
